clean_story:
Neural pathways. Practice reshapes them.

"Repetition" comes from the Latin repetere—to seek again. Like a trail through tall grass, each repeated action flattens the path further, making it easier to follow.

Every skill starts as a struggle. The first time you tie your shoes, your brain fires wildly, recruiting entire regions just to loop a lace. But repeat it daily, and those neural signals narrow into a single, efficient route—like upgrading from a dirt road to a highway. This is synaptic pruning: unused connections wither while active ones strengthen, wrapped in fatty myelin for faster signals. Pianists’ brains show thicker motor cortex areas for finger control; taxi drivers grow larger hippocampi (memory centers) from navigating the same streets.

Even emotions rewire this way. Rehearse anxiety, and your amygdala’s fear circuits become hypersensitive. Replace that with deep breathing, and new calming pathways dominate. Repetition isn’t just memory—it’s physical reconstruction.

Three related subjects are muscle memory mechanics, habit formation loops, and neuroplasticity after injuries.
